questions.question: List four types of sensors commonly used in IoT devices and give one application for each.
keyPoints: Temperature sensors (thermistors, thermocouples) — environmental monitoring, HVAC control
Motion sensors (PIR, accelerometers) — security systems, occupancy detection
Light sensors (photodiodes, LDRs) — smart lighting, agricultural applications
Pressure sensors — industrial automation, weather stations, healthcare devices

questions.question: Compare and contrast microcontrollers and microprocessors in IoT, with examples of each.
keyPoints: Microcontrollers (MCUs) integrate processor, memory, and I/O peripherals on a single chip. Examples: Arduino Uno, ESP32, STM32.
Microprocessors (MPUs) contain only the CPU and require external memory, storage, and peripherals. Examples: Raspberry Pi (Broadcom SoC), BeagleBone, NVIDIA Jetson.
MCUs are suited for constrained IoT devices due to low cost, low power, and compact size.
MPUs are suited for gateway devices and edge computing where full OS support, complex processing, and higher computational power are needed.

questions.question: Compare any four wireless communication technologies used in IoT based on range, power consumption, and typical use cases.
keyPoints: Bluetooth/BLE: Short range (10-100m), low power. Used for wearables and personal device sync.
Wi-Fi: Medium range (50-100m), high power, high data rate. Used for home automation and high-bandwidth applications.
LoRaWAN: Long range (2-15 km), very low power, low data rate. Used for smart agriculture, smart cities.
Cellular (4G/5G): Very long range (nationwide), high power. Used for vehicles, remote monitoring where Wi-Fi/LoRa coverage is unavailable.

questions.question: Explain the physical design considerations for deploying IoT devices in an industrial environment. Include at least four factors.
keyPoints: Environmental durability — devices must withstand extreme temperatures, humidity, vibration, dust, and EMI/RFI interference.
Power considerations — reliable power sources, battery backup, energy harvesting (solar, vibration), and power management techniques like duty cycling.
Communication reliability — industrial-grade protocols, redundancy in communication paths, and interference-resistant technologies.
Physical security — tamper-proof enclosures, secure boot mechanisms, hardware security modules to prevent unauthorized access.
Maintenance and cost — modular design for easy component replacement, accessible mounting, and total cost of ownership analysis.
